textauszug: interior courtyard, but also in reforming the layout of the facade.34 By varying the design for each storey and introducing traditional house elements, such as gables, balconies, bays and pitched roofs, he achieved a six- storey tenement which looked like a single house. He therefore translated the picturesque—just transferred from the English Arts and Crafts model to Berlin’s suburbs under the inﬂuence of Hermann Muthesius— onto a metropolitan scale and provided the usual Mietskaserne with a new sense of home. Proud of his own achievements, he published many of his buildings under the title The German Tenement in 1909. 35 Gessner’s buildings also provide a perfect example of the long-lasting success of this typology combined with good design and construction quality. Flats in these buildings are among the most popular and achieve the highest prices on the property market. What is more, they have been popular throughout the century, even in times when most planners and architects were promot- ing other typologies. Furthermore, being designed with quite generic ﬂoor plans and sufﬁcient room heights, they also allow for conversion into ofﬁce spaces and back. They have therefore proven their adaptability to urban changes which cannot be foreseen, and thus constitute a sustainable model as—even if major functional changes occur—no larger construction process is needed. The typology of enlarged courtyards was able to adopt quite different characters. While Gessner’s houses aimed to domesticate the tenements, there were other examples whose intention was to underline the serial character of metropolitan architecture. This tendency was outlined in B. Leibnitz’s Boarding Palast in the Kurfu¨rstendamm (1913), where a large number of small ﬂats were arranged next to long corridors, surrounding three large interior courts and covered by a metropolitan facade towards the street. 36 Apart from the basic form of the large green courtyard, a variety of solutions to reform the urban block emerged in Berlin. One of these typologies was the arrangement of internal streets through the privately owned block. Examples from speculative developments were Goethepark by Paul Geldner and Andreas V oigt (1902–03), or Verso ¨hnungs-Privatstrasse in Berlin- Wedding (1904). 37 These private internal streets were often only accessible through archways and also contained elements of enlarged backyards. Paul Mebes designed the famous buildings in Fritschweg in Berlin- Steglitz (1907–08) for the housing cooperation of the W. Sonne / Progress in Planning 72 (2009) 53 –149 63 Fig. 5. Albert Gessner, apartment houses on Bismarckstraße and Grolmannstraße in Berlin-Charlottenburg, 1906–07. 34 Posener, J. (1979). Berlin auf dem Wege zu einer neuen Archi- tektur. Das Zeitalter Wilhelms II (pp. 321–327). Munich: Prestel. 35 Gessner, A. (1909). Das deutsche Miethaus. Ein Beitrag zur Sta¨dtekultur der Gegenwart. Munich: Bruckmann. 36 Posener, J. (1979). Berlin auf dem Wege zu einer neuen Archi- tektur. Das Zeitalter Wilhelms II (pp. 335–337). Munich: Prestel. 37 Geisert, H. (2000). Models for the reform of urban housing. In: Kahlfeldt, P., Kleihues, J. P., & Scheer, T. (Eds.),City of architecture. Architecture of the city Stadt. Berlin 1900 –2000 (pp. 40–51, 48). Berlin: Nicolai.
